summary: The q6 dataset shows uniform activity across all ten problems (q1-q10), each carrying exactly 100 issues with no resolution differentiation provided. The flat distribution and absence of proof-status granularity prevent identification of standout traction or near-complete proofs from the current data alone.
problems: All problems (q1-q10) report an identical 100 open issues, giving no signal about relative activity or traction
No proof-status breakdown (open vs. resolved, blocking vs. minor) is available, so solvability cannot be assessed from the data
The uniform 100-issue count suggests a possible data cap, placeholder values, or unparsed metadata rather than true issue counts
Dataset-specific context for q6 versus other problems is missing, limiting targeted prioritization
highlights: Complete coverage: every problem in the dataset has tracked issues, indicating consistent monitoring
Standardized issue counts make the dataset easy to ingest and compare once richer metadata is added
No problem appears abandoned — all show active issue tracking

suggested_todos.title: Verify whether the uniform 100-issue count reflects real data or a reporting cap/placeholder
Enrich issue records with status fields (open/resolved, severity, blocking) to enable solvability ranking
Add per-problem activity timestamps to surface which problems have recent momentum
Define proof-status categories so 'likely solvable soon' can be computed objectively
Generate a follow-up report once differentiated metadata is available
